EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sunset Fire started on August 13 from undetermined cause. It was located 26 miles North of Coeur d'Alene.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sunset Wildfire started on August 13 by unknown human cause. It was located 26 miles North of Coeur d'Alene. It burned 3183 acres of timber. Three structures were lost. It contained on September 2. Cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning Creek Fire started on July 30 by lightning. It was located 9 miles north of Clark Fork Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning Creek Fire started on July 30 by lightning. It was located 9 miles north of Clark Fork. It continued through the month of September. It burned 2482 acres of timber, brush, and grass. Cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful winter storm brought a combination of heavy snow and very strong winds which caused road closures in eastern Idaho.

Idaho Falls recorded a 64 mph wind on ASOS with 60 mph at Ucon and 58 mph at Osgood.  A vehicle crash on interstate 14 north of Osgood blocked southbound lanes at 6 AM.  In Rexburg, by 3 pm, Pole line Rd was closed from S 5th W to Millhollow Rd due to strong winds.  Four miles south of Plano, Idaho Highway 33 closed at 315 pm from S 5000 W to S 4000 W due to a crash involving a semi trailer with a power pole.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple waves of subtropical moisture brought periods of moderate to heavy precipitation, primarily as snowfall across the Northern Rockies. Nightly bouts of black ice, often covered by fresh snow, created extremely slick travel conditions.|Arctic air remained stalled along and just west of the Divide, occasionally pushing westward. These surges intensified snowfall, produced below-zero wind chills, and brought gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy snow caused power outages in the northeastern part of the Camas Prairie. Total snowfall ranged from near 9 inches in Grangeville up to 16 inches in Woodland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 0.25 and 1.00 inch of rain fell at lower elevations across north-central Idaho which caused black ice issues Friday night. Heavy snow fell in the mountains with over a foot of accumulation towards the Bitterroot Spine. This event started January 31.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lolo Pass received 14 inches of heavy snow during the event. Freezing rain caused slick surfaces in the lower elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between 0.25 and 1.00 inch of rain fell at lower elevations across north-central Idaho which caused black ice issues Friday night. Heavy snow fell in the mountains with over a foot of accumulation towards the Bitterroot Spine. This event started January 31.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 11th, a shortwave trough crossed across southwest Idaho aiding in thunderstorm development. RAP Mesoscale Analysis showed precipitable water values between 0.8 - 1 inch, around the climatological 95th percentile. With this moisture, heavy rain over burn scars was a primary hazard with storms that developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Forest Service official reported that Forest Service Road 555 (Scott Mtn Rd) was washed out as a result of a stream of water ran over the road. The radar estimated rainfall total was 0.3 inches, with a maximum estimated rainfall rate of 2 inches per hour. Timing was estimated from peak hydrophobic unit flow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm developed as moisture from the southwest overran an arctic cold front pushing in from the northeast, resulting in significant impacts across north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho County Sheriff's Office declared emergency travel only for U.S. Highways 95 and 12 due to winter driving conditions on February 4 at 1805PST.  They reported 'several accidents' and stated that visibility was limited due to snow.  The power company reported 692 customers without power on February 5 at 0337PST.  Lolo Pass SNOTEL received 1.30 inches of snow-water equivalent, likely corresponding to well over a foot of snow accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm developed as moisture from the southwest overran an arctic cold front pushing in from the northeast, resulting in significant impacts across north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho County Sheriff's Office declared emergency-only travel for U.S. Highways 95 and 12 on February 4 at 1805 PST. They reported 'several accidents' due to snow.  One power company reported 1,239 customers without power on February 4 at 2222PST. The CoCoRAHS site at 3 NNE Clearwater reported 18 inches of snow on February 5 at 0800PST, while another site reported 10 inches at 5 NNE Orofino. The Grangeville COOP recorded 13 inches of snow with 1.18 inches of precipitation. Kamiah and Grangeville schools were closed on February 5.

They also reported a semi crashed on U.S. Highway 95, milepost 196 at 1803PST on February 4.  A trained spotter on Whitebird Hill measured 18 inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked across southern Idaho and brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to Idaho and Lemhi Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Grangeville COOP reported 4.8 inches of snowfall, while the airport recorded north winds between 10 and 13 mph. The webcams showed snow-covered roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A winter storm developed as moisture from the southwest overran an arctic cold front pushing in from the northeast, resulting in significant impacts across north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow fell initially, with up to 10 inches of accumulation near Gibbonsville and 8 inches in Salmon. During the day, melting snow caused water to accumulate on roadways, which turned to ice after dark due to ground temperatures remaining well below freezing. A weather spotter northeast of Carmen reported that all side streets were icy. He measured 7 inches of snow with 0.55 inches of water between February 2-3. Lemhi County also reported icing on county roads. Lost Trail Pass recorded 24 inches of snow in the  48-hour period ending at 0500MST on February 5. A snow squall produced wind gusts up to 45 mph and heavy snow around 1000MST on the 5th, impacting northern Lemhi County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked across southern Idaho and brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to Idaho and Lemhi Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine to ten inches of snow were reported on Williams Creek Summit. Given that high elevation stations like the Red Rock Peak RAWS recorded westerly winds of 15 to 31 mph, blowing and drifting snow were likely occurring at the summit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked across southern Idaho and brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to Idaho and Lemhi Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Idaho Transportation Department (ITD) reported 10 to 12 inches of snow at Whitebird Grade and 3 to 4 inches towards Riggins. ITD cameras showed heavy snow accumulating on U.S. Highway 95 between 0600 and 0730PST. Riggins also experienced tree damage and power outages that lasted 12 to 24 hours after the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low tracked across southern Idaho and brought periods of moderate to heavy snow to Idaho and Lemhi Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lemhi County Roads Department described the storm as a 'high-end event'. They reported 3 inches of snow overnight in Salmon, followed by another 4 inches between 0600 and 1000, for a storm total of 7 inches. Snow falling on already icy roads made travel very difficult, prompting the declaration of emergency-travel-only status. They also reported a 30-minute period of intensified winds, resulting in near-zero visibility. Additionally, a road grader flipped on the ice, though the driver was not injured.

The Paradise Creek in Moscow crested at 11.21 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm sequence, initially targeting the central ID mountains and Upper Snake Highlands with heavy snow. Strong wind gusts and accumulating snow then moved into eastern Idaho where the southeast highlands saw accumulating snow with slippery road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A long period of snowfall brought 6 to 12 inches of snow through Island Park and Ashton Hill, with 12 to 24 inches north of Island Park towards Targhee Pass and West Yellowstone. Island Park measured 15.3 inches of snow, while the White Elephant SNOTEL measured nearly 45 inches of snow during the long duration event. Additionally, wind gusts occasionally reached 50-55 mph with a measured gust of 51 mph reported at the Henry's Lake ITD mesonet site. The combination of heavy snow and strong wind gusts led to the closure of Highway 20 from Ashton to the Montana border beginning the afternoon of the 16th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Sand Creek Fire was started by lightning and spread rapidly on August 15th.  The fire was 8 miles west of Ashton in the Sand Creek area, near Lower Arcadia Reservoir.  Strong winds spread the fire rapidly.  Evacuations were ordered for those living in the area by the 16th.  This affected residents in the Cedar Ridge Subdivision including the surrounding 5 mile radius near Sadorus Hill.  Structures were threatened and one outbuilding was destroyed.  The fire covered 851 acres and burned grass, brush, juniper, aspen and conifer.  It was 100 percent contained by the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sand Creek Fire was started by lightning and spread rapidly on August 15th.  The fire was 8 miles west of Ashton in the Sand Creek area, near Lower Arcadia Reservoir.  Strong winds spread the fire rapidly.  Evacuations were ordered for those living in the area by the 16th.  This affected residents in the Cedar Ridge Subdivision including the surrounding 5 mile radius near Sadorus Hill.  Structures were threatened and one outbuilding was destroyed.  The fire covered 851 acres and burned grass, brush, juniper, aspen and conifer.  It was 100 percent contained by the 18th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A system brought enough instability to produce scattered showers across the region. A weak funnel cloud from one of the showers was reported to have occur over Kootenai County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotter KO160 witnessed a cold air funnel from the intersection of Hanley Rd and North Atlas Road. Funnel cloud looks to have formed from a shower southwest of the intersection based on available radar data. No Damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture surged into the region over a three day period, the most intense of which was the 26th. High atmospheric moisture content combined with weak flow in a monsoonal pattern to produce very moist storms moving very slowly. Additionally, as terrain served as the main trigger for convection, storms would train over the same spots for much longer than normal. Precipitable water was at near record levels around 1.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On the east side of the Valley Fire burn scar, heavy rainfall with a peak rain rate of 7 inches per hour caused a slurry of mud, ash, and debris to flow down eastern Highland Valley Rd, depositing nearly a foot of debris on the road with running water estimated at 12 inches high. The road would have been impassable while water was running, and the debris flow ran down the length of the valley. No impacts along Hwy 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture surged into the region over a three day period, the most intense of which was the 26th. High atmospheric moisture content combined with weak flow in a monsoonal pattern to produce very moist storms moving very slowly. Additionally, as terrain served as the main trigger for convection, storms would train over the same spots for much longer than normal. Precipitable water was at near record levels around 1.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall rates on the recent Valley Fire burn scar created a debris flow that ran down the entire length of Highland Valley, half of which flowed into the Boise River and the other half into a canal. The slurry heavily impacted a community along Warm Springs, running water/slurry could have been anywhere from 3 to 8 ft in height, swept a truck into a fence, only being caught by other debris. Lawns were covered in debris and a white picket fence would have been submerged in the flow. This came from a storm with a peak rain rate of 7 inches per hour that remained over Lucky Peak for an extended period of time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture surged into the region over a three day period, the most intense of which was the 26th. High atmospheric moisture content combined with weak flow in a monsoonal pattern to produce very moist storms moving very slowly. Additionally, as terrain served as the main trigger for convection, storms would train over the same spots for much longer than normal. Precipitable water was at near record levels around 1.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following the debris flow that impacted the community at the bottom of Highland Valley, the half of the flow that entered the canal followed the canal into another community near Barber. The flow was so high it crested a canal culvert and began flowing down streets of the suburban community. Multiple inches of silt were built up in the neighborhood, with debris at every turn along the way. Triggering a fast response clean up to reopen the neighborhood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture surged into the region over a three day period, the most intense of which was the 26th. High atmospheric moisture content combined with weak flow in a monsoonal pattern to produce very moist storms moving very slowly. Additionally, as terrain served as the main trigger for convection, storms would train over the same spots for much longer than normal. Precipitable water was at near record levels around 1.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A training thunderstorm persisted near Arrowrock Dam for about 45 minutes with rain rates between 3 and 6 inches per hour. Rocky cliff faces and steep valleys allowed water to accumulate, though soil/brush retention absorbed most of the rain. While Arrowrock Road was still drivable afterwards, there was evidence of significant water erosion, with multiple spots estimated to have at least 6 inches of running water. The road would have been undrivable during the event.

Additionally, as terrain served as the main trigger for convection, storms would train over the same spots for much longer than normal. Precipitable water was at near record levels around 1.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small amount of water in a recently burned area with steep slopes allowed standing water to accumulate to 3 feet next to Hwy 21 near MP 80. This occurred around the same time as mudslides and rockslides on the same highway. While radar captured very little precipitation, this area was known to be susceptible to runoff, especially with the recent wild fires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning Creek Fire started on July 30 by lightning. It was located 9 miles north of Clark Fork Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning Creek Fire started on July 30 by lightning. It was located 9 miles north of Clark Fork. It continued through the month of August. It burned 2482 acres of timber, brush, and grass. Cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved through the Sandpoint area on August 29. It brought severe hail, gusty winds and brief heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three reports of one inch hail were recorded for a strong thunderstorm for Dover and Sagle Idaho near Sandpoint. The reports came from a trained spotter and two from the public including a mPing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sunset Fire started on August 13 from undetermined cause. It was located 26 miles North of Coeur d'Alene.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sunset Wildfire started on August 13 by unknown human cause. It was located 26 miles North of Coeur d'Alene. It burned 3183 acres of timber. Three structures were lost. It continued into September. Cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Damaging winds from nearby thunderstorms struck the town of Leadore. These winds were produced by significant evaporative cooling, which occurred as rain fell from high clouds into very dry air near the surface.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Destructive thunderstorms swept through the Leadore area, unleashing estimated wind gusts of 80 mph. A local motel and a double-wide mobile home had their roofs ripped off by the violent winds. The storm also toppled trees, damaged home siding, pushed over 300 feet of fencing, and rolled a 300-gallon fuel tank.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowfall occurred during the morning hours as a closed low passed south of the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall reports of 4 to 8 inches were common for Leadore and Gilmore Summit.  Ten inches of snow occurred at North Basin, located just north of Watson Peak. While paved roads remained clear overall, backcountry routes were snow-covered.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 9th, an upper level low tracked northeast across southwest Idaho aiding in thunderstorm development. With a dry boundary layer, and marginal precipitable water values, outflow winds were the primary hazard with burn scar flooding a secondary hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station IDARNG2_RG14 measured a wind gust of 63 mph as a thunderstorm outflow passed. This outflow continued into Elmore county. No damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 9th, an upper level low tracked northeast across southwest Idaho aiding in thunderstorm development. With a dry boundary layer, and marginal precipitable water values, outflow winds were the primary hazard with burn scar flooding a secondary hazard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At 6:30pm MDT, Idaho Power mesonet station 032ID recorded a wind gust of 68 mph. Later, at 6:36pm MDT, a RAWs (LLCI1) recorded a gust of 59 mph. These gusts were associated with a outflow boundary from a thunderstorm southwest of both locations. No damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On July 31st, a shortwave trough embedded in southwesterly flow aloft moved over southwestern Idaho. This forcing, paired with increasing moisture in the southwesterly flow, allowed for scattered thunderstorms to develop across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Deer Haven RAWS recorded a gust of 50 knots associated with a thunderstorm outflow.

Despite the intensity of the storm, no lightning occurred ��� an unusual feature for the region. No vehicles were trapped.

A section of siding was blown off a residence where a carport also blew over 2 miles east southeast of Idaho Falls. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture surged into the region over a three day period, the most intense of which was the 26th. High atmospheric moisture content combined with weak flow in a monsoonal pattern to produce very moist storms moving very slowly. Additionally, as terrain served as the main trigger for convection, storms would train over the same spots for much longer than normal. Precipitable water was at near record levels around 1.2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain amounts of 0.2-0.3 inches in an hour caused a debris flow that trapped cars near MP 103 of Hwy 21. This surprisingly small amount of water produced a mudslide because of two important reasons. First, the Wapiti burn scar last year burned very intensely in the flood location, leaving lots of ash and debris on the ground. Second, this portion of Hwy 21 is notorious for avalanches because of it's steep, fairly sparse forest. The very steep open slopes that were primed with ash allowed the small amounts of water to run off almost immediately, carrying debris and mud with it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Medicine Lodge Creed near Small went into flood stage around 950 PM on the 24th and reached an all time record at 1230 AM on the 25th and didn't drop below flood stage until 545 AM on the 25th.   Two road washouts occurred.   One was near the convergence of Medicine Lodge and Middle Creeks.  The second was a mile further into the canyon where the Medicine Lodge Creek was flowing over the road.  Four to five families were affected by the washouts.  Twelve hour rainfall amounts ranged in the 1 to 2 inch range in the flood area.  The highest amount was 1.81 inches 9 miles northwest of Small.   The flooding area was 3 to 10 miles northwest of Small.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Our very last thunderstorm event of the year happened as a strong cold front moved through the region, replacing the warmer late summer airmass with a proper fall airmass. This dropped snow levels behind the event, bringing just below an inch of rainfall right before multiple inches of snow fell on the mountains. Winds aloft near 40-60 kt matched gust front winds from a line of showers and storms accompanying the front, resulting in localized severe impacts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage cut off power to 3790 people in the Horseshoe Bend, Crouch, and Placerville area as winds along a gust front moved through. Damage estimated to be 60 mph based on power pole damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent atmospheric river moved into the Northern Rockies between December 4 and 7, delivering significant mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Between 6 and 7 inches of snow fell between Elk City and Dixie, while the mountains, including both Lolo Pass and Red River Hot Springs, saw much heavier totals of 1 to 2 feet. Precipitation changed to freezing rain at Elk City during the evening of December 5, but air temperatures rose above freezing by midnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent atmospheric river moved into the Northern Rockies between December 4 and 7, delivering significant mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of light to moderate snow resulted in widespread minor accumulations across the lower elevations, including 1.8 inches in Cottonwood, 3.5 inches near Orofino, and 4.4 inches in Woodland. As the system progressed, snow transitioned to rain, causing temperatures to fluctuate and potentially leading to the formation of black ice on road surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent atmospheric river moved into the Northern Rockies between December 4 and 7, delivering significant mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 1.5 inches in Salmon, to 6.7 inches in Gibbonsville, to over a foot of snow at Lost Trail Pass. Snowfall changed to rain in the Salmon area at night while low temperatures were below freezing which implies black ice issues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 17, 2025, a historic high wind event driven by a strong cold front undercutting an atmospheric river brought hurricane-force winds to North Central Idaho. Saturated, extremely wet soils severely weakened root structures, which substantially exacerbated the toppling of countless trees and power poles. The region experienced devastating wind gusts exceeding 70 mph.|The storm caused catastrophic damage to utility infrastructure, resulting in what Clearwater Power described as the most widespread outage and system damage the cooperative has ever experienced. The intense winds and falling timber damaged equipment, substations, and every single main distribution line across their expansive 5,000-square-mile electric system all at once. Fallen trees completely blocked roads and access points, while additional snow and wind following the initial event severely hampered repair progress. Outages persisted for over a week for some residents, with crews and mutual aid partners quadrupling the normal workforce to work through the Christmas holiday to fully restore power across the rugged landscape.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dent RAWS, located at 1,700 feet, reported wind gusts up to 55 mph between 0508PST and 0608PST, and 57 mph between 0608PST and 0708PST. Fallen trees completely blocked roads and access points throughout the county. Widespread tree damage was noted across central and eastern Clearwater County, including North Fork Road, which was completely blocked by fallen timber between Quartz Creek and Washington Creek. |The storm caused severe, localized utility impacts. At 0710PST on December 17, 2,613 out of 5,960 tracked customers (nearly 44 percent) were without power in Clearwater County. Across Clearwater Power's broader network, power was eventually restored to over 10,750 members, but at least 250 members remained without power through the Christmas holiday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 17, 2025, a historic high wind event driven by a strong cold front undercutting an atmospheric river brought hurricane-force winds to North Central Idaho. Saturated, extremely wet soils severely weakened root structures, which substantially exacerbated the toppling of countless trees and power poles. The region experienced devastating wind gusts exceeding 70 mph.|The storm caused catastrophic damage to utility infrastructure, resulting in what Clearwater Power described as the most widespread outage and system damage the cooperative has ever experienced. The intense winds and falling timber damaged equipment, substations, and every single main distribution line across their expansive 5,000-square-mile electric system all at once. Fallen trees completely blocked roads and access points, while additional snow and wind following the initial event severely hampered repair progress. Outages persisted for over a week for some residents, with crews and mutual aid partners quadrupling the normal workforce to work through the Christmas holiday to fully restore power across the rugged landscape.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An Idaho Transportation Department weather station at Concrete Section recorded a peak gust of 75 mph at 0700PST and multiple instances of sustained winds between 40 and 44 mph between 0600 and 0815PST. The top of Greer Grade measured a 73 mph gust between 0600PST and 0615PST. The Idaho County Sheriff's Office reported that most county roads were blocked or partially blocked by downed trees. Travel was highly discouraged, and official travel not recommended advisories were issued by 1100PST. Emergency responders dealt with multiple highway blockages, including State Highway 13 which was completely shut down between mileposts 6 and 11 due to power lines lying across the roadway, as well as large rocks and trees, and the stretch between Orofino and Peck was described as horrible with trees down everywhere.||In Cottonwood, police reported extreme damage including missing roofs and doors blown completely off a Primeland grain elevator. Downed trees crushed a parked car on 3rd Street, and blocked both King and Myrtle streets, and another massive tree crashed through the roof of a house just moments after a child had left their bed. Near Orofino, a large tree branch pierced the roof of a trailer home in Hidden Village, displacing the residents who were subsequently assisted by the Red Cross. In Grangeville, shingles were blown off roofs, and between Fenn and Tolo Lake, the wind lifted a 40x60 foot open alley barn, moving it 6 to 8 feet off its foundation.||Nearly 4,000 customers lost power in the Idaho County area alone, with regional afternoon totals exceeding 4,600 without power. Broken phone poles, large branches in power lines, and widespread infrastructure damage led to prolonged, multi-day outages, with over 1,000 Clearwater Power customers still without electricity by the evening of December 19. Due to the severe hazards and power loss, numerous regional school districts were forced to cancel classes, including Grangeville, Kamiah, Cottonwood, Orofino (SD 171), and several private academies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-December's synoptic regime was dominated by a potent atmospheric river that caused widespread flooding on the west coast and persistent precipitation in the intermountain west. Forcing was largely provided by a stationary front along the river, with shortwaves in the mid-level flow allowing it to change from warm to cold front every few days as it kept active weather in the area. However, a deep mid-level trough would strengthen on the West Coast, and as it made landfall December 17th and 18th it pushed a surface low of 980 Mean Sea Level Pressure with winds at 700 millibars of near 100 knots. The strong cold front associated with the surface low, strong winds in the low-levels from the Pressure Gradient Force of the low and the atmospheric river, and the persistent moisture supply from the atmospheric river was the perfect environment for severe wind outbreak across southeast Oregon and southwest Idaho.||Unlike typical stratiform winter passages, this event featured embedded convective showers and thunderstorms. This convective element enhanced the transport of high-momentum air to the surface. It was noted that the atmospheric remained fully coupled throughout the event, a very rare occurrence in mid-December. The frontal passage brought winds of near 60 miles per hour across the area, while the morning squall along the front saw winds up to 80 miles per hour, with one ridgeline observation of over 100 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of trees down across the Western Magic Valley as pre-frontal, squall line, and post-frontal severe winds moved through. A roof was ripped off in Bliss. Two pine trees fell down on a house in Hazelton. Downed trees blocked roads in Jerome and Twin falls scattered debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-December's synoptic regime was dominated by a potent atmospheric river that caused widespread flooding on the west coast and persistent precipitation in the intermountain west. Forcing was largely provided by a stationary front along the river, with shortwaves in the mid-level flow allowing it to change from warm to cold front every few days as it kept active weather in the area. However, a deep mid-level trough would strengthen on the West Coast, and as it made landfall December 17th and 18th it pushed a surface low of 980 Mean Sea Level Pressure with winds at 700 millibars of near 100 knots. The strong cold front associated with the surface low, strong winds in the low-levels from the Pressure Gradient Force of the low and the atmospheric river, and the persistent moisture supply from the atmospheric river was the perfect environment for severe wind outbreak across southeast Oregon and southwest Idaho.||Unlike typical stratiform winter passages, this event featured embedded convective showers and thunderstorms. This convective element enhanced the transport of high-momentum air to the surface. It was noted that the atmospheric remained fully coupled throughout the event, a very rare occurrence in mid-December. The frontal passage brought winds of near 60 miles per hour across the area, while the morning squall along the front saw winds up to 80 miles per hour, with one ridgeline observation of over 100 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public posted a picture of a 6 inch diameter branch broken, as well as a large 1.5 foot diameter section of the tree snapped. Winds estimated to have been 60 miles per hour based on state and type of tree, at least severe given the size of the large branch that snapped off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 17, 2025, a historic high wind event driven by a strong cold front undercutting an atmospheric river brought hurricane-force winds to North Central Idaho. Saturated, extremely wet soils severely weakened root structures, which substantially exacerbated the toppling of countless trees and power poles. The region experienced devastating wind gusts exceeding 70 mph.|The storm caused catastrophic damage to utility infrastructure, resulting in what Clearwater Power described as the most widespread outage and system damage the cooperative has ever experienced. The intense winds and falling timber damaged equipment, substations, and every single main distribution line across their expansive 5,000-square-mile electric system all at once. Fallen trees completely blocked roads and access points, while additional snow and wind following the initial event severely hampered repair progress. Outages persisted for over a week for some residents, with crews and mutual aid partners quadrupling the normal workforce to work through the Christmas holiday to fully restore power across the rugged landscape.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A falling tree struck a semi-truck at milepost 96 on US-12, just one mile west of Lowell. In an accident near Elk City, a truck was struck by mud and trees sliding down a hillside, while a motorist on SH-13 had a tree fall through their vehicle windshield. Blockages including multiple downed trees, rocks and landslides were reported along State Highway 14. ||Utility infrastructure was devastated, leading to massive and prolonged power outages. By 0637PST on December 17, over 27 percent of tracked customers in Idaho County (1,638 meters) had lost power. To address the widespread outages, Avista deployed more than 45 line crews, 36 contract crews, and 30 vegetation management crews, supported by hundreds of additional employees. Through this massive response, 86 percent of Avista accounts across the region were restored by December 18. Restoration efforts for remote locations like Elk City and Dixie, as well as the Cove area east of Grangeville, safely extended into subsequent days.||Property and infrastructure damage extended deep into the backcountry. At Red River Hot Springs, multiple trees were reported down. Public lands agencies reported significant storm-related damage to facilities. The Nez Perce-Clearwater National Forests noted catastrophic damage at the Fish Creek Recreation Area outside Grangeville, where multiple trees crushed the pit toilet building, pavilion, and parking lot.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow brought light snow and slick travel across north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals ranged from 3.8 inches in Pierce to 4 to 6 inches in the mountains. Road temperatures were above freezing before snow began which could have brought localized slick spots.

Wind gusts recorded were 76 mph 2 miles northeast of Pingree, 75 mph 2 miles north of Neely, 74 mph in Aberdeen, 72 mph in Fort Hall, 61 mph in Chubbuck, 64 mph at the Pocatello Regional Airport ASOS, 59 mph at Blackfoot and Taber, and 64 mph near Sterling.  ||Some of the damage reports received were:  Several large trees downed in Aberdeen.  Aberdeen was the focal point of much damage including over 150 power poles snapped off or downed with irrigation wheel damage.    In the American Falls area 21 mature trees downed along Fairview Road and North Pleasant Valley Road.  20 power poles were snapped off 5 miles northwest of American Falls and Povey Road impassable.  A large structure damaged on Michaud Road east of American Falls.   In Pocatello multiple trees downed and power lines down on Hancock and Fern roads east of Alameda Road closing the roads.  Numerous trees and power lines downed throughout the city with fence damages as well in the Pocatello and Chubbuck areas.   ||A tree was blown onto a pickup truck in Fort Hall.   Power outages in parts of Aberdeen and American Falls continued for multiple days with American Falls and Aberdeen schools used as warming centers for those without power.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totals included 2.2 inches at Woodland and 1.5 inches in Cottonwood. Road temperatures at Greer Grade were 36 degrees before snow began at 1530PST on December 1, creating a risk for localized slick spots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy snow and strong winds to the Clearwater and Bitterroot Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho side of the Bitterroot crest absorbed heavy moisture from the atmospheric river, leading to high-impact winter conditions right along the Lolo Pass border. Further down the Highway 12 corridor at Powell, intense snowfall rates dropped 5 inches of snow in just a 6-hour period during the afternoon and evening of December 18. In Dixie, 6.5 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy snow and strong winds to the Clearwater and Bitterroot Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Salmon, the COOP observer reported 5 inches of snowfall. Lost Trail Pass saw periods of heavy snow and blowing snow at times. The Moose Creek SNOTEL, located just south of the pass, reported 14 inches of snow depth increase during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mid-December's synoptic regime was dominated by a potent atmospheric river that caused widespread flooding on the west coast and persistent precipitation in the intermountain west. Forcing was largely provided by a stationary front along the river, with shortwaves in the mid-level flow allowing it to change from warm to cold front every few days as it kept active weather in the area. However, a deep mid-level trough would strengthen on the West Coast, and as it made landfall December 17th and 18th it pushed a surface low of 980 Mean Sea Level Pressure with winds at 700 millibars of near 100 knots. The strong cold front associated with the surface low, strong winds in the low-levels from the Pressure Gradient Force of the low and the atmospheric river, and the persistent moisture supply from the atmospheric river was the perfect environment for severe wind outbreak across southeast Oregon and southwest Idaho.||Unlike typical stratiform winter passages, this event featured embedded convective showers and thunderstorms. This convective element enhanced the transport of high-momentum air to the surface. It was noted that the atmospheric remained fully coupled throughout the event, a very rare occurrence in mid-December. The frontal passage brought winds of near 60 miles per hour across the area, while the morning squall along the front saw winds up to 80 miles per hour, with one ridgeline observation of over 100 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southern Owyhee County RAWS sites measure instantaneous gusts of 58-67 miles per hour. While likely a slight overestimate given the instantaneous calculation of gusts, observations in surrounding zones are consistent with severe winds gusts throughout the southwest highlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through Idaho Panhandle early morning on December 17. It brought gusts in the range 60 to 72 mph. Numerous of reports of downed trees and powerlines. One fatality was reported in the Fernan Lake Village area from tree falling on house. Other minor structure damage was reported as some trees fell on houses.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station 9654 Parker Pass recorded a gust of 60 mph 4 miles Southeast of Chatcolet at 0700 LST. A semi truck was blown on its side near Moscow. Downed trees blocked US 95 South of Plummer. Moscow area had several trees and powerlines downed. Damage cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An atmospheric river brought heavy rains to the Central Idaho Panhandle December 10 and 11. Areas saw 48 hour rain amounts of 2 to 4 inches. The rain combined with snow melt and led to several flooding and landslide incidents. An embedded cold front brought high winds up to 60 mph to the region causing downed trees and power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station 8484 D2 at Shirrod Hill recorded a gust of 60 mph at 0215 LST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowmobile trail maintenance crews covered the Pilot Peak area shortly after the new year, the first time checking on the trail since before this event. Maintenance crews reported an astonishing number of trees down along the trails, and were quickly overwhelmed by the volume of cleanup required. There were hundreds of trees down on trails with already low snow, so many trails had to be closed. The crew reported that they would be unable to cut them all this year, and multiple feet of snow would be required to fully bury the trees.

The strong cold front associated with the surface low, strong winds in the low-levels from the Pressure Gradient Force of the low and the atmospheric river, and the persistent moisture supply from the atmospheric river was the perfect environment for severe wind outbreak across southeast Oregon and southwest Idaho.||Unlike typical stratiform winter passages, this event featured embedded convective showers and thunderstorms. This convective element enhanced the transport of high-momentum air to the surface. It was noted that the atmospheric remained fully coupled throughout the event, a very rare occurrence in mid-December. The frontal passage brought winds of near 60 miles per hour across the area, while the morning squall along the front saw winds up to 80 miles per hour, with one ridgeline observation of over 100 miles per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho Transportation Department's Jerome Butte automated weather station observed a 62 mph wind gust coincident with the passage of a line of showers and embedded thunderstorms. Tree debris was strewn in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent atmospheric river moved into the Northern Rockies between December 4 and 7, delivering significant mountain snowfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The period of highest impact occurred between 0900PST and 1515PST on December 4, when snowfall rates near 1 inch per hour frequently reduced visibility to less than one mile. The Grangemont area received an estimated 8 inches of new snowfall during that period. The snow changed to freezing rain at night in the lower elevations. The surrounding mountains received 8 to 14 inches of new snowfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful cold front ripped through eastern Idaho on the 17th and included severe thunderstorms along the front and extremely high non thunderstorm winds in the cold sector post cold frontal passage throughout the day.   Extensive damage was reported throughout the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree uprooted and power pole downed in Paul.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A front moved the region bringing strong winds to the lower portion of the Idaho Panhandle. Wind gusts ranged from around 30 to 50 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station ITD58 at Shirrod Hill recorded a gust of 53 mph. Power outages were reported in the in the St Maries and Hayden areas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station ITD00 at Lake Creek US-95 recorded a gust of 50 mph. Power outages were reported in the in the St Maries and Hayden areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow impacted the mountains of north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel measured 7 inches of snowfall at Crater Meadows, 5 inches at Hemlock Butte, and 3 inches at Elk Butte.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow impacted the mountains of north-central Idaho.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snotel reported 3 inches of snowfall at Lolo Pass, 3 inches at Mountain Meadows, and 6 inches at Savage Pass.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: No significant weather event occurred for the North Idaho Panhandle. A few small wildfires were started. One fire was intentionally set by an individual that ambushed first responders. He fatally shot two. The rest of the fires did not burn a reportable size or did not cause significant damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nettleton Gulch Fire was started around 1220 PST on June 29 Eat of Hayden, Idaho. It was a deliberate human caused fire. The individual then fired on first responders causing 2 fatalities and one injury. It continued until July 5 and burned 23 acres of timber and grass. Cost is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse convection and thunderstorms produced localized areas of nearly stationary, intense rainfall and hail ranging from pea sized to 1 inch in diameter during the afternoon and evening of September 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A hail size of one inch in diameter was reported 3 miles north-northwest of Kamiah by a trained spotter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pulse convection and thunderstorms produced localized areas of nearly stationary, intense rainfall and hail ranging from pea sized to 1 inch in diameter during the afternoon and evening of September 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Idaho County Sheriff's Office Facebook page reported washouts and subsequent closure on Reservation Line Road near Cottonwood Creek due to heavy rainfall from thunderstorms. A Flood Advisory for the area was issued on the evening of the 11th. The Sheriff's office reported that the road reopened by 1320 PST on the 12th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure brought clear skies and dry air, and this allowed overnight temperatures to drop near freezing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures dropped to 30-33 degrees at Elk City and Powell for the first time in the fall season leading to significant frost and damage to area gardens. Frosty mornings continued through the rest of the week.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire burning grass and sage began September 28th about 6 miles north of Ketchum.   It continued through the end of the month and grew to 512 acres.  It was near the Lake Creek picnic area and had Blaine County issuing pre evacuation notices but no evacuations occurred.   It was finally contained the first week of October.
